back_translation: At that time, some people came to report to Him concerning the Galileans whose blood Pilate had mixed with their sacrifices.
He answered them, «Do you think that these Galileans were more sinful than all the Galileans because they had to suffer like this?
I tell you, it is not so, but if you do not repent, all of you will perish in the same way.
Or those eighteen people on whom the tower at Siloam fell and killed them — do you think they were more guilty than all the people residing in Jerusalem?
I tell you, it is not so, but if you do not repent, all of you will perish in the same manner.»
Then He told this parable: «A certain man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard, and he came looking for fruit on it but did not find any.
So he said to the vineyard-worker, ‹Look, for three years I have been coming to look for fruit on this fig tree but I do not find any. Cut it down! Why let it make the ground useless?›
The man answered, ‹Master, please leave it alone this year also, until I dig around it and apply animal manure,
and if next year the tree bears fruit, all is well; but if not, you shall cut it down.›»
On a Sabbath day, He was teaching in one of the synagogues.
Look, there was a woman who had been made sick by a spirit for eighteen years; her back was bent over and she was not able to straighten herself up at all.
When Jesus saw her, He called her over and said to her, «Woman, you are released from your sickness.»
Then He laid His hand on her, and immediately she straightened up and began to praise God.
But the synagogue ruler was displeased that Jesus had healed on the Sabbath, so he said to the crowd, «There are six days for work. Therefore come to be healed on those days, not on the Sabbath.»
The Lord answered him, saying, «You hypocrites! On the Sabbath does not each of you untie his ox or donkey from the stall and lead it out to give it water?
Then this woman, who is a daughter of Abraham, whom Satan had bound — behold, eighteen years now — was it not fitting for her to be released from this bondage on the Sabbath day?»
When He spoke these things, all those who opposed Him received shame, and the whole crowd rejoiced in all the glorious works that He did.
Therefore He said, «What is the kingdom of God like? And to what shall I compare that kingdom?
That kingdom is like a mustard seed that a man took and sowed in his garden; the seed sprouted and became a large tree, until birds of the air came to make nests in its branches.»
And He said again, «To what shall I compare the kingdom of God?
That kingdom is like leaven that a woman took and mixed into three measures of flour, until the whole lump rose.»
He went through the cities and villages, teaching and journeying toward Jerusalem.
Someone said to Him, «Lord, are those who will be saved few?» He said to them,
«Strive to enter through the narrow door, for I tell you, many people will try to enter but will not be able.

Once the master of the house has risen up and shut the door, you will begin to stand outside and knock at the door saying, ‹Master, please open for us.› And he will answer you, ‹I do not know you; where are you from?›
Then you will begin to say, ‹We ate and drank in your presence, and you taught in our streets.›
But he will answer you, ‹I do not know where you are from. Depart from my presence, all you who do unrighteousness.›
There shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth, when you see Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and all the prophets in the kingdom of God, but you yourselves being thrown out.
And people will come from the east and the west, from the north and the south, to recline at table in the kingdom of God.
And look, there are last ones who will become first, and there are first ones who will become last.»
At that very time, some Pharisees came to Him and told Him, «You should leave this place, because Herod wants to kill you.»
He said to them, «Go tell that fox, ‹Look, today and tomorrow I cast out demons and heal illnesses, and on the third day I will complete my work.›
But I must continue traveling today, tomorrow, and the next day, because it is impossible for a prophet to be killed outside Jerusalem.

O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, you who kill the prophets and stone those who are sent to you, how often have I longed to gather your children together, just as a hen gathers her young under her wings, but you did not want it.
Behold, your house is left to you. And I tell you, you will not see me again, until the time comes when you say, ‹May the one who comes in the name of the Lord be blessed.›»
